elements.sent-2411-001: P\'al's isominwidth theorem states that for a fixed minimal width, the regular triangle has minimal area.
elements.sent-2411-002: A spherical version of this theorem was proven by Bezdek and Blekherman, if the minimal width is at most $\tfrac \pi 2$.
elements.sent-2411-003: If the width is greater than $\tfrac \pi 2$, the regular triangle no longer minimizes the area at fixed minimal width.
elements.sent-2411-004: We show that the minimizers are instead given by the polar sets of spherical Reuleaux triangles.
elements.sent-2411-005: Moreover, stability versions of the two spherical inequalities are obtained.
elements.sent-2411-006: The (minimal) width $w(K)$, or sometimes also referred to as thickness, of a convex body $K\subset\R^n$, i.e., of a compact convex set in $\R^n$, is commonly defined as the minimum distance of two parallel supporting hyperplanes of $K$.
elements.sent-2411-007: Back in 1921, P\'al studied the relation between the area and the width of a 2-dimensional convex body $K\subset\R^2$.
elements.sent-2411-008: He proved that if the width $w$ is fixed, the unique (up to isometry) convex body $K\subset\R^2$ of minimal area is the regular triangle whose width is $w$ \cite{Pal .}
elements.sent-2411-009: This beautiful result may be regarded as a dual version of the isodiametric inequality, which states among all convex bodies $K\subset\R^n$ of fixed diameter $D$, the Euclidean $n$-balls of diameter $D$ uniquely maximize the volume \cite[Eq.\ (7.22)]{Sch14 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: While the isodiametric inequality holds in all dimensions, a higher-dimensional analog of P\'al's result is not at hand.
elements.sent-2411-011: The problem of determining among the convex bodies $K\subset\R^n$ for fixed width $w$ the ones of minimal volume is known as P\'al's problem, or as the isominwidth problem.
elements.sent-2411-012: Apart from the Euclidean setting, an isominwidth (or isodiametric) problem may be posed in each kind of geometry that allows for a definition of width (diameter) and volume.
elements.sent-2411-013: For spaces of constant curvature the isodiametric problem has been solved by Schmidt \cite{Sch48,Sch49 and B\"or\"oczky, Sagmeister \cite{BoS20,BoS23 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: On the 2-sphere $S^2$, the isominwidth inequality has been proven by Bezdek and Blekherman \cite{Bez00 for width $w\leq\frac{\pi {2}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: The spherical width functional $w(K)$ is defined in Section \ref{subsec:width .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Moreover, equality holds in the spherical isominwidth problem only if $K$ is congruent with $T_{w}$ (cf.\ Section \ref{sec:pal:smallw ).}

elements.sent-2411-048: For a pair of non-antipodal points $x,y\in S^n$, the unique geodesic segment connecting $x$ and $y$ is denoted by $\left[x,y\right]$.
elements.sent-2411-049: The spherical distance of $x$ and $y$ is denoted by $d\left(x,y\right)$ and the diameter of $X$ is denoted by $\diam X$.
elements.sent-2411-050: We call a set $X\subseteq S^n$ convex, if it is contained in an open hemisphere and for each pair of points $x,y\in X$, we have $\left[x,y\right]\subseteq X$.
elements.sent-2411-051: Similarly to Euclidean convexity, intersection preserves convexity on the sphere too, so we can define the convex hull of a subset $X$ of an open hemisphere as the intersection of all spherically convex sets containing $X$ denoted as $\conv X$.
elements.sent-2411-052: If $X = \{x_1,\dots, x_k\}$ is a finite set, we also write $[x_1,\dots,x_k]=\conv\{x_1,\dots,x_k\}$.
elements.sent-2411-053: A set $X\subseteq S^n$ is called a convex body if it is convex, compact, and its interior is not empty.
elements.sent-2411-054: Spherical caps (balls in $S^n$ with respect to the spherical metric) are denoted as $B\left(x,r\right)$ where $x$ is the center of the ball, while $0<r\leq\pi$ denotes its radius.
elements.sent-2411-055: We note that balls of radius $r$ are convex if and only if $r<\frac{\pi {2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-056: A notion of width in $S^n$ is defined with lunes by Lassak\cite{La15 .}
elements.sent-2411-057: A lune is an intersection of two different closed hemispheres of the unit sphere $S^n$ whose centers are not antipodal.
elements.sent-2411-058: The boundary of a lune consists of two $(n-1)$-dimensional hemispheres that intersect in an $(n-2)$-sphere.
elements.sent-2411-059: Given a lune $L\subset S^n$, the breadth $b(L)$ of $L$ is the geodesic distance of the centers of the two $(n-1)$-dimensional hemispheres bounding $L$.
elements.sent-2411-060: For a spherically convex body $K\subset S^n$ the lune $L$ is a supporting lune if $K\subseteq L$ and $K$ intersects both hemispheres bounding $L$.
elements.sent-2411-061: The width of a spherically convex body regarding a supporting hyperplane $H$ is the minimal breadth among all the supporting lunes to $K$ such that one of the bounding $\left(n-1\right)$-dimensional hemispheres of these supporting lunes is contained in $H$; we use the notation $w_K\left(H\right)$ for this quantity.
elements.sent-2411-062: Lassak showed \cite{La15 , that the spherical width function is continuous, so for a convex body both the maximal and the minimal width are attained.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The (minimal) width of a convex body $K\subset S^n$ is \begin{equation}w\left(K\right)=\min\left\{w_K\left(H\right)\colon H is a supporting hyperplane to K\right\}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-065: A reduction of a convex body $K$ is a reduced convex body $L\subseteq K$ with $w\left(L\right)=w\left(K\right)$.
